utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Ladies and gentlemen, at this time, please place all electronic devices, all electronic devices to vibrate.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Madam Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías

chapters.summary: Reverend Patrick J. Keating, Deputy CEO of Catholic Charities Brooklyn and Queens, delivers the invocation. He prays for the city council, first responders, and vulnerable New Yorkers, while also honoring the memory of Pope Francis.
chapters.title: Invocation by Reverend Patrick J. Keating

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: We will now have today's invocation, which will be delivered by Reverend Patrick J.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Keating, Deputy CEO of Catholic Charities Brooklyn and Queens, and the CEO of Catholic Migration Services, which is located at 310 Prospect Park West in Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Reverend Patrick J. Keating
utterances.text: Almighty God, we pray that you send your blessing upon the members of the City Council.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Reverend Patrick J. Keating
utterances.text: We pray also for the dedicated staff of this illustrious chamber and all in government.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Reverend Patrick J. Keating
utterances.text: We pray for your protection upon the brave and dedicated women and men of the NYPD, the FDNY, and all first responders.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Reverend Patrick J. Keating
utterances.text: We pray for all civil servants and those who partner with the city, especially sober service providers.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Reverend Patrick J. Keating
utterances.text: And we also pray for all New Yorkers who strive to make our city a better place for all people.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Reverend Patrick J. Keating
utterances.text: During the Octave of Easter, a time when Christians remember the central mystery of our faith, namely the resurrection, we pray that the joy of this season might remind each of us to be concerned with the needs and to treat our sisters and brothers with mercy, love, and compassion.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Reverend Patrick J. Keating
utterances.text: We pray that all people of goodwill strive for the common good, as we each do what we can to uplift one another.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Reverend Patrick J. Keating
utterances.text: We are mindful of the passing of His Holiness Post Francis on Easter Monday.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Reverend Patrick J. Keating
utterances.text: The Holy Father graced the City of New York with a papal visit in 2015, a visit in which he focused on the 25th chapter of St.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Reverend Patrick J. Keating
utterances.text: Matthew's Gospel, reminding all of us of the need to feed the hungry, the need to care for the sick, the need to welcome the stranger, and the need to accompany those who feel abandoned and may be on the periphery of society.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Reverend Patrick J. Keating
utterances.text: For more than 125 years, Catholic Charities of Brooklyn and Queens, along with many other not-for-profits in the city of New York, have strived to feed the hungry, to care for the sick, to help the vulnerable, as well as to give hope to our neighbors.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Reverend Patrick J. Keating
utterances.text: We are mindful that we can only succeed by working together, especially with our partners in government.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Reverend Patrick J. Keating
utterances.text: We pray that this council continue to deliberate and focus their efforts to help and assist the most vulnerable forgotten in our city.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Reverend Patrick J. Keating
utterances.text: Throughout his ministry, Pope Francis reminded the world that every single person has value and dignity.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Reverend Patrick J. Keating
utterances.text: He called for the Jubilee year of hope, a time when our world, our nation, and our city needs hope.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Reverend Patrick J. Keating
utterances.text: We pray that all of us be agents of hope, that we may treat one another with respect and dignity, that we may raise one another up in mutual concern and in the realization that we all call to help one another.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Reverend Patrick J. Keating
utterances.text: Let us never forget that genuine kindness can truly change the world.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Reverend Patrick J. Keating
utterances.text: Lastly, we pray for those who are sick and those who have lost loved ones.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Reverend Patrick J. Keating
utterances.text: May your compassion uplift their hearts to focus on the hope that comes from your divine providence.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Reverend Patrick J. Keating
utterances.text: We ask this in all things to our Lord Jesus Christ, your son, in union with the Holy Spirit, God forever and ever.

utterances.speaker: Member of the Public - Reverend Patrick J. Keating
utterances.text: Amen.

chapters.summary: Council Member Justin Brannan thanks Reverend Keating for the invocation and pays tribute to the legacy of Pope Francis, highlighting his advocacy for the poor and marginalized. Brannan moves to spread the invocation in full upon the record.
chapters.title: Motion to Spread Invocation on the Record and Tribute to Pope Francis

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Thank you, Reverend Keating.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I would now ask Councilmember Justin Brennan to spread the invocation on the record.

utterances.speaker: Justin L. Brannan
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Justin L. Brannan
utterances.text: Thank you, Reverend Keating, for your invitation.

utterances.speaker: Justin L. Brannan
utterances.text: The very Reverend Patrick J.

utterances.speaker: Justin L. Brannan
utterances.text: Keating is a devoted servant and leader of the Roman Catholic Diocese of Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Justin L. Brannan
utterances.text: He served as deputy CEO of Catholic Charities Brooklyn and Queens and CEO of the Catholic Migration Services.

utterances.speaker: Justin L. Brannan
utterances.text: Reverend Keating, you lead with compassion every day, and this council thanks you for it.

utterances.speaker: Justin L. Brannan
utterances.text: It's an honor to have the Reverend with us today as we join the world in mourning the passing of Pope Francis, a spiritual giant, and a relentless advocate for the poor, and a moral compass for so many.

utterances.speaker: Justin L. Brannan
utterances.text: Pope Francis reminded the world that faith and action means standing with the poor, the marginalized, and the forgotten.

utterances.speaker: Justin L. Brannan
utterances.text: His legacy is one of humility, compassion, challenging the status quo, and speaking uncomfortable truths to power, and never losing sight of the dignity in every single human being.

utterances.speaker: Justin L. Brannan
utterances.text: The legacy that Pope Francis leaves us is not just words, but a challenge to do better, to be better for the least among us, for each other.

utterances.speaker: Justin L. Brannan
utterances.text: It's not just about going to church on Sunday, it's about what you do and how you treat others during the rest of the week.

utterances.speaker: Justin L. Brannan
utterances.text: Before you talk about your faith, show it to us and how you treat other people.

utterances.speaker: Justin L. Brannan
utterances.text: May his memory be a blessing and may we rise to meet the call he's left behind.

utterances.speaker: Justin L. Brannan
utterances.text: Madam Majority Leader, I thank once again thank the very Reverend Patrick J.

utterances.speaker: Justin L. Brannan
utterances.text: Keating, and I make a motion for unanimous consent to spread the invocation in full upon the record.

utterances.speaker: Justin L. Brannan
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Speaker Adrienne Adams begins her communications by acknowledging the tragic death of Exenia Met, a beloved Harlem grandmother who was killed by a stray bullet while trying to stop neighborhood violence.
chapters.title: Speaker Adams Pays Tribute to Exenia Met

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Thank you, Madam Majority Leader, and good afternoon, everyone.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Before we begin, I want to acknowledge the senseless death of Exenia Met, also known as Zini, who was struck in the head by a stray bullet as she rushed outside of her apartment in Harlem to try to stop the violence she heard.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Zini was a grandmother known throughout her neighborhood as a pillar of the community, and our heart breaks at the loss of life for this innocent bystander who wanted nothing more than to stop the violence.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: And the survivors who are working to recover from the effects of the fire.

chapters.summary: Speaker Adams addresses the deportation of Bronx teenager Maruel Gutierrez by the Trump administration. She criticizes Mayor Eric Adams for his silence on the matter and warns against allowing ICE operations on Rikers Island.
chapters.title: Speaker Adams Condemns Trump Administration Deportation and Mayor's ICE Policy

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: I also want to address the disturbing reports of the Trump administration essentially kidnapping a Bronx teenager, Maruel Gutierrez, and sending him to El Salvador, to which he has no connection, even after acknowledging he was not the person they were looking for.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: His father is now left concerned about his son, who he and his lawyers have indicated has no criminal history and no gang affiliations or ties.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: And as a mother and grandmother, I can only imagine the fear and anger his family is experiencing at this grave injustice.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: This isn't yet another example of the Trump administration's extreme agenda that disregards the U.S.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Constitution's due process.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: The mayor claims that he supports due process and that his record on this is clear, but he hasn't said anything about this, and sadly, none of us should be surprised.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: His behavior this week as a subsidiary of the Trump administration showed his willingness to spread misinformation that even contradicts the Supreme Court multiple times.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: His actions this week seem to demonstrate that he really is fulfilling his bargain with Donald Trump.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: But even more, their press conference undermined their claim of the necessity to have an office for ICE because they can clearly collaborate on criminal enforcement without it.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: The only logical conclusion is that the plan is to violate the law by enacting civil immigration enforcement if given the keys to Rikers.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: The mayor has placed his own personal interests ahead of the people of our city.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: The same due process that he says he supports applies to most people on Rikers Island.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Allowing ICE on Rikers would undermine safety in our city, it would lead to more of the same constitutional violations that we're already seeing from the Trump administration against New Yorkers and people across our city and nation.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: And when New Yorkers are afraid of cooperating with our city's own police and discouraged from reporting crime or seeking help, it makes everyone in our city less safe.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: The council will continue to stand firm to protect the rights and safety of all New Yorkers against the Trump administration, even if the mayor will not.

chapters.summary: Speaker Adams reflects on the significance of Passover, Easter, Earth Day, Holocaust Remembrance Day, and the upcoming Denim Day to support survivors of sexual violence.
chapters.title: Speaker Adams Recognizes Holidays and Heritage Days

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Passover provides us the opportunity to reflect on our history and instill these lessons in our children.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: I hope everyone who celebrated had a meaningful holiday.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Last Sunday was also Easter Sunday.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Easter signifies hope and renewal, and I hope New Yorkers who celebrated were able to come together as a community to reflect on our shared values of compassion, unity, and resilience.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: April 22nd was Earth Day, which serves as an important call to action with urgency to protect our planet.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: We must continue our work to achieve a zero-waste future and build upon our green infrastructure so that New York City can do its part to fight climate change.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Yesterday was Holocaust Remembrance Day, honoring the six million Jewish people who were killed during the Holocaust.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: We must continue our work to be vigilant against hate and work to preserve and teach our history so that it's never again repeated.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: And looking ahead, April 30th is Denham Day, a day committed to supporting survivors of sexual violence and increasing public awareness about this important issue.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: It's a day of action where we show our collective solidarity for survivors around the world and recommit to supporting survivors with the trauma-informed care and recovery they need.

chapters.summary: Speaker Adams welcomes young adults from JCCA's Arches program to the chambers and wishes happy birthdays to Council Members Mercedes Narcisse and Chris Marte.
chapters.title: Speaker Adams Welcomes JCCA's Arches and Wishes Happy Birthdays

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Next, please join me in welcoming a group of young adults from JCCA's arches to Council Chambers.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: We're proud of you and glad that you could join us today.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: And I want to wish an early happy birthday.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: I believe she just left Council Member Mercedes Narcisse, whose birthday, and Councilmember Chris Marte, whose birthday is on the 26th.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Happy birthday.

chapters.summary: Speaker Adams outlines the stated agenda, starting with the Brownsville NCP affordable housing project and several finance items, including a bill regarding the Independent Budget Office's executive budget report.
chapters.title: Speaker Adams Outlines Land Use and Finance Agenda Items

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Now let's move on to our stated agenda.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: First, we'll vote on the following land use items: Brownsville, NCP, an application by HPD to facilitate the development of deeply affordable housing on three parcels of vacant city-owned land in Councilmember Nurse and Councilmember Mealy's districts.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: All together, the three buildings will include 75 units with affordability ranging from 30 to 70 percent of the AMI.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Next, we'll vote on the following finance items.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Introduction 1234A, sponsored by Councilmember Justin Brann, would require require the director of the Independent Budget Office to publish a report analyzing the mayor's executive budget for the ensuing fiscal year on May 20th of each year, as opposed to May 15th.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Additionally, this bill would move the council's public hearings on the mayor's executive budget to between May 10th and May 31st.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Introduction 1219, sponsored by Deputy Speaker Diana Ayala, would establish the East Harlem 125th Street Business Improvement District.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Resolution 327, sponsored by Councilmember Sandy Nurse, would call for passage of state legislation to authorize the city to offer retroactive tax exemptions.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: And preconsidered resolution, sponsored by Councilmember Justin Brann, to approve new designation and changes in the designation of certain organizations to receive funding in the expense budget.

chapters.summary: Speaker Adams details home rule item SLR1 regarding Flushing Meadows Corona Park, a resolution on industrial laundry contracts, and a charter amendment regarding former public servants.
chapters.title: Speaker Adams Outlines Home Rule and General Legislation Agenda Items

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Next, we'll vote on the following home rule.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: SLR1 would authorize the city to discontinued the use of certain designated parkland within Flushing Meadows Corona Park and dedicate an amount equal to or greater than the fair market value towards acquiring new parklands and or capital improvements of existing parklands with a minimum of 20 acres.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: This condition, this conditional based on approval and development of a gaming facility along with commercial retail, entertainment, recreational, and community facility uses.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Today, we'll also vote on the following pieces of legislation.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Resolution 598, sponsored by Councilmember Carmen Della Rosa, would call on the New York City health care institutions to contract with industrial laundry companies that respect workers' legal rights and adhere to area standards for wages and benefits.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Thank you to our staff member Elizabeth Arts.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: A preconsidered introduction, sponsored by Councilmember Lincoln Wrestler, would amend the New York City Charter to account for numbering changes in a previous law and preserve the right of former public servants to communicate with city agencies or government offices as part of an appearance in a legal proceeding in front of another agency office or court.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Thank you to our staff members.

chapters.summary: Speaker Adams highlights a package of bills to combat deed theft and predatory real estate practices, as well as legislation requiring reporting on community hiring programs by local development corporations.
chapters.title: Speaker Adams Outlines Deed Theft and Community Hiring Legislation

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Next, we will vote on a pair of bills to combat deed theft and its predatory practices that stem from my 2024 state of the city.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Introduction 889A, sponsored by Councilmember Crystal Hudson, would require that the system maintained by the Department of Finance provide notification to interested parties of the recording of certain real estate instruments, also notify the local council member when it affects a property that has been held by the same party for 30 years.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Introduction 1086A, sponsored by Councilmember Nantasha Williams, would require the DOF to notify interested parties by email, text message, and postal mail when a deed or mortgage-related document has been recorded for the property in 30 days or less.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Next, introduction 860A, sponsored by Councilmember Alexa Aviles, would require local development corporations to report annually on community hiring programs established to promote employment opportunities for residents living in low-income communities during the prior fiscal year.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: The report shall be posted on the New York City Economic Development Corporation's website.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: And introduction 861A, also sponsored by Councilmember Aviles, would alter the terms of the contract between the city and EDC to require it to include details regarding agreements between developers and a nonprofit organization or person to provide community benefits as part of a development project.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Thank you to our staff members, Rachel Cordero and William Hongak.

chapters.summary: Speaker Adams discusses legislation to increase stormwater system capacity and a package of bills and resolutions aimed at regulating non-essential helicopter flights to mitigate noise pollution.
chapters.title: Speaker Adams Outlines Stormwater and Helicopter Noise Legislation

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Next, we will vote on legislation aimed at increasing capacity of the city's stormwater system.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Resolution 144A, sponsored by Councilmember Julie Menon, calls on the New York State Environmental Facilities Corporation to remove restrictive barriers and uncapped funds New York City can receive for water infrastructure upgrades.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Thank you to our staff member Andrew Bourne.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Resolution 8, sponsored by Councilmember Jim Gennaro, calls on the New York State Department of Health and New York State Environmental Facilities Corporation to fairly allocate grants and loans for lead service line replacement approved in the federal bipartisan infrastructure law and to remove all rules preventing New York City from receiving a fair share of this funding.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Thank you to our staff members, Ricky Chola and Natasha Bynum.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Introduction 115A, also sponsored by Councilmember Gennaro, would require DEP to develop a plan to increase the capacity of the stormwater system to convey rainfall.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Additionally DEP would be required to display the status of the comprehensive stormwater plan and of all recommended projects and policies recommended on an online dashboard.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Thank you, staff members Andrew Bourne and Sierra Townsend.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Next, we'll vote on a package of legislation to regulate non-essential helicopter flights with the goal of mitigating noise pollution and its impacts on health and quality of life.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: During the past five years, there's been a 2,329% increase in noise complaints related to helicopters, as well as recent helicopter-related incidents earlier this month.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: We lost Sean Johnson when the helicopter he was piloting crashed into the Hudson River, resulting in his death and the loss of a family of five.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Resolution 233A, sponsored by Councilmember Gail Brewer, calls on the Federation Federal Aviation Administration to ban all non-essential helicopter travel, including tourist and chartered helicopter flights over New York City.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Resolution 226A, sponsored by Councilmember Gail Brewer, calls on the New York State Legislature to pass and the governor to sign A.6311, S.7381, which would prohibit certain non-essential flight operations at municipal heliports and Hudson River Park.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Resolution 85A, sponsored by Councilmember Gennaro, calls on the New York State Legislature to pass and the governor to sign.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: A.5891, S.1140, which would establish a noise tax on non-essential helicopter and seaplane flights in cities with a population of one million or more.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Thank you to our staff member William Hongak.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: And introduction 26A, sponsored by Majority Leader Amanda Farrias, would prohibit helicopters that do not meet the most stringent FAA noise standards from operating for non-essential purposes out of the East 34th Street Heliport and the downtown Manhattan Wall Street Heliport beginning at the end of 2029.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Additionally, the bill would require EDC to report on the percentage of flights at these two heliports that do not currently meet the noise standards that will be enforced in 2029.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Thank you to our staff members, Rachel Cordero, William Hongak, David Seitzer, and Alex Puladoff.

chapters.summary: Speaker Adams introduces a package of bills and resolutions designed to protect and support transgender, gender nonconforming, and nonbinary New Yorkers against federal rollbacks.
chapters.title: Speaker Adams Outlines Transgender and Gender Nonconforming Protection Legislation

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Finally, we'll vote on several bills aiming to improve resources and ensure protections for transgender, gender nonconforming, and nonbinary New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: These actions are even more critical as the Trump administration continues to threaten the civil rights of trans and gender nonconforming people across the nation.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Resolution 793, sponsored by Councilmember Lynn Shuman, calls on calls on the New York State Legislature to pass and the governor to sign.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: S.1633, A.2613, which would provide additional reproject uh additional protections for the sensitive health information of patients, including information related to abortion or gender-affirming care.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Resolution 781, also sponsored by Councilmember Shulman, would call on the New York State Legislature to introduce and pass and the governor to sign.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Legislation to prohibit out-of-state access to the New York State prescription monitoring programs data for certain medications, including abortion medications, hormone therapy, and puberty blockers.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Resolution 774, sponsored by Councilmember Hudson, calls on the New York State Department of Health to implement regulations requiring hospitals in New York City to adhere to the World Professional Association for Transgender Health Standards of Care 8 to ensure medical professionals can provide high quality and ethical treatment for individuals with gender dysphoria.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Resolution 7071, sponsored by Councilmember Eric Botcher, calls on the New York State Governor to sign S.929A.2141, which would extend protections over personal health information.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Thank you to our staff member Christy Dwyer.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Introduction 246A, sponsored by Councilmember Hudson, would require agencies that provide social services and collect demographic information through form documents from residents seeking services to update their forms, where practicable, to include X as an option when gender identity information is collected.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Thank you to our staff members, Jayasri Kenopathy, Erica Cohen, and Rachel Cordero.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Introduction 1204A, sponsored by Councilmember Chi Osei, would require the city's health commissioner to conduct an assessment on health inequities affecting transgender, gender non-conforming, non-binary, and intersex individuals.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: DOHMH would be required to develop a health agenda to address these inequities.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Introduction 1203A, also sponsored by Councilmember Ose, would require the Mayor's Office of Immigrant Affairs to conduct an assessment of the available resources and any service gaps in the city for newly arrived migrants who are transgender, gender non-conforming or non-binary and intersex.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Introduction 1201A, sponsored by Councilmember Shahana Hanif, would expand a person's private right of action for interference with reproductive or endocrine medical care to explicitly include all forms of gender reforming care.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: The bill would take effect immediately.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: And introduction 1200A, spell sponsored by Councilmember Tiffany Caban, would require the Mayor's Office of Equity and Racial Justice to provide public publicly available information on the legal rights and resources available to transgender, gender non-conforming, and non-binary individuals.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: The bill would also require the cities to submit a report on the public information, provided and engage in a media campaign regarding this public information.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Thank you to our staff members Julia Goldsmith Pinkham, Christy Dwyer, and Sahar Mozami.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Thank you all for your attention.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: Now I turn it back over to our majority leader.

chapters.summary: Council Member Sandy Nurse speaks in support of the Brownsville NCP project, which will bring 75 units of deeply affordable housing to Community Board 16, highlighting the inclusion of three-bedroom units.
chapters.title: Council Member Nurse Discusses Brownsville NCP Housing Project

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Thank you, Speaker Adams.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: We will now move into discussion of general orders, and I'd like to first recognize Councilmember Hani, followed by Nurse, then Kaban.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Will you actually come back to me?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Calling on Councilmember Nurse.

utterances.speaker: Sandy Nurse
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Sandy Nurse
utterances.text: I just wanted to share some words of support for the Brownsville NCP project that will bring 75 units of much needed affordable housing to community board 16.

utterances.speaker: Sandy Nurse
utterances.text: 55% of CB uh 16 residents are rent burdened and spend over 35% of their income on rent and are in desperate need of affordable housing options.

utterances.speaker: Sandy Nurse
utterances.text: Mark's Avenue, the largest of the three buildings, will be in my district, and will bring 39 affordable apartments below 70% of area medium income.

utterances.speaker: Sandy Nurse
utterances.text: 21% will be set aside for homeless families, and 50% will be set aside for families making below 60% of AMI.

utterances.speaker: Sandy Nurse
utterances.text: The three bedrooms will be affordable to a family of four, with two parents making up to $54,000 each.

utterances.speaker: Sandy Nurse
utterances.text: These kinds of units are harder and harder to find, and I appreciate the Fifth Avenue Committee and HPD for responding to the community's desire to see more three-bedroom projects in this district, which was also supported by a slight density bonus of five units from the City of Yes zoning text change.

utterances.speaker: Sandy Nurse
utterances.text: I want to also especially thank HPD, the team there for really accommodating a number of changes, overcommunicating, and helping us achieve a very good project for this community.

utterances.speaker: Sandy Nurse
utterances.text: I ask for your support on this item today.

utterances.speaker: Sandy Nurse
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader.

chapters.summary: Council Member Tiffany Cabán urges colleagues to pass the transgender rights package, emphasizing that New York City must actively lead in protecting the survival and rights of trans residents.
chapters.title: Council Member Cabán Urges Support for Transgender Rights Legislation

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Recognizing Councilmember Caban, followed by Aviles.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Yes, I would like to talk about the Brownsville and Councilmember Millie.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Oh have not recognized you yet.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I recognize Councilmember Caban.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Go come on.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Today I am urging my colleagues to support and pass intro 1200 and the entire bill package.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: It's a step towards liberation and justice for our transgender and gender nonconforming, non binary and intersex neighbors.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: At a time when the rights of trans people are under attack across the country, it is not enough for cities like ours simply to say, we stand beside you.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: We must act and we must lead.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: The legislation is part of that effort.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: It mandates that the city create a resource hub and conduct a know your rights campaign to make critical life-saving information readily available to the trans and gender non-conforming community.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: This includes information about their rights under the law, how to access gender-affirming care, and where to find support from community organizations.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: It ensures that people know how to change their name and gender markers, and that they understand the protections that exist from hate crimes and discrimination.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: This bill is about transparency, accessibility, and solidarity.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: But most importantly, it's about survival.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: We must be clear that the passage of this bill is not the finish line.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: It's just the start of what we must do.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: The Trump administration's rollback of health care protections, the targeting of trans youth, and the attempts to erase trans people from public life are reminders that our work is just beginning.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: New York City must continue to be a national leader, not just in resisting harm, but in actively advancing the rights and safety of TGNCNBI people.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I want to thank the advocates and community leaders who fought to support this legislation, many of whom have spent years fighting for recognition and basic rights.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: We must uplift the leadership of TGNCNBI people at every level of government.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Alexa Avilés advocates for her bills, Intros 860 and 861, which aim to increase transparency and accountability regarding community hiring and benefits in EDC-led economic development projects.
chapters.title: Council Member Avilés Discusses Community Hiring and EDC Accountability Bills

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Recognizing Councilmember Aviles, followed by Gennaro.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: I'm urging my colleagues to vote yes on two pieces of legislation, intro 860 and 861.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Both of these bills get to the heart of challenges faced by my community when economic development projects led by our city are dropped on us without care, consideration, or involvement.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Intro 860 will require entities that contract with the EDC to publish an annual report concerning community hiring.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: For years, we have heard all EDC make promises to our communities, touting an app an opportunity for significant economic and workforce development opportunities, and have yet to see so many of those promises materialize, including the thousands of jobs promised through the Made in New York project.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Intro 860 will make transparent key measurements around community hiring, including performance metrics for a community hiring compared to previous fiscal years, and a description of efforts undertaken by the contracted entity to employ community members.

utterances.speaker: Alexa Avilés
utterances.text: Through this legislation, we will have an ability to better monitor and understand how projects may contribute to or extract from our local growth and development.

chapters.summary: Council Member Jim Gennaro discusses Intro 115A, which requires the DEP to develop a plan to increase stormwater sewer capacity to combat the increasing frequency of severe flooding events.
chapters.title: Council Member Gennaro Discusses Stormwater Management Legislation

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Recognizing Councilmember Gennaro, followed by Hanif.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Thank you, Madam Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Uh happy Earth Week, everyone.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: I want to talk about intro 1130, and that's uh about um uh storm sewers.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: This is a follow-up to local law five of 2008, which is the city that which I authored, which is the city's first um comprehensive stormwater management plan.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Uh that plan we had sewer we we had storm sewer problems back then, and the idea of that uh of that plan was to emphasize bioretention and green roofs and uh tree pit design and rain gardens and blue belts just to sort of maximize like the natural way to get rid of stormwater.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Um unfortunately we're uh at a time now uh where that is not going to get it done.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Uh so we're gonna do now that which we didn't do then, which is actually increase the capacity of the sewers.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Uh currently the sewers are are built to absorb about one point seven 1.75 inches of rain per hour, which is which was a pretty good standard uh once upon a time.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: That accounts for a five-year storm, uh, but now five year storms happen five times a year.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Um so uh last year we passed local law 124, the inland rainfall risk area maps to make sure that we highlighted that that uh um DEP was gonna highlight where all the hot spots are in terms of flooding.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Uh and then this plan, which will uh which will uh play out over 10 years just to get the plan together, um, is gonna focus on those uh hot spots, you know, uh make those priorities, uh, figure out what kind of stormwater capacity we can actually put under the street, figure out how to pay for it, uh, and then it's gonna play out over decades to actually get it in place because we have more than 7,000 miles of um uh storm sewer main.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: So uh that uh uh storm sewer main.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: So buckle up.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: It's gonna be a long ride, but it's gonna be a good one.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: At the end of the day, we're gonna have a more sustainable, flood-free city.

utterances.speaker: James F. Gennaro
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

chapters.summary: Council Member Shahana Hanif speaks in support of Intro 1201, the Affirm Act, which establishes safe harbor protections for individuals accessing gender-affirming care in New York City.
chapters.title: Council Member Hanif Discusses the Affirm Act for Gender-Affirming Care

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Recognizing Councilmember Honey, followed by Vernakoff, then Mealy.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Today we are voting on my bill intro 1201, the Affirm Act.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: I'd like to thank Brooklyn Borough President Reynoso and Council members Botcher, Rivera Ose, Wrestler Hudson, and Delarosa for introducing this bill with me.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Women and gender equity chair Lewis for moving it through the committee, and Speaker Adams for including it on today's agenda.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: I'm proud that this council is stepping up to fight Donald Trump's trans-erasure agenda.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: The Protect Trans Futures Package is a bold suite of legislation that makes it loud and clear that trans and gender non-conforming people have rights here, no matter who is in the White House.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: As other jurisdictions pass anti-trans legislation, the Affirm Act creates safe harbor protections for those who access gender-affirming care here in the five boroughs.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: These patients will be shielded from lawsuits from anti-trans governments or entities that are filed on the basis of gender-affirming care that is provided in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: If a suit is filed, it shall constitute interference, and the aggrieved patient will have the personal ability to counter sue for damages and other costs under city law.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: This bill protects gender affirming care and affirms our city's values as a bastion for TGNC rights.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: I urge my colleagues to vote yes.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: I'd like to thank Alex Liao, Moose Lamia, Nisha Djodhri, and Matt Maloy from my team, Jahari Frazier, Julia Goldsmith Pinkham, and Sahar Mozami from the legislative division, and the incredible coalition of issue experts who got the Affirm Act to this point.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: I'll close by saying that as important as the suite of legislation is, there is still much the city needs to do to further trans rights, including allocating and baselining the following funds in the FY26 budget.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: 10 million for the trans equity initiative, 15 million for gender affirming care, and 10 million for LGBTQ youth housing.

utterances.speaker: Shahana K. Hanif
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Inna Vernikov voices strong opposition to the transgender rights legislative package, arguing that it infringes on parental rights and compromises fairness in women's sports.
chapters.title: Council Member Vernikov Opposes Transgender Rights Legislation

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Recognizing Councilmember Vernikov, followed by Mili.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: The package of bills and resolutions being pushed through today is riddled with language that will negatively affect the rights of parents to be involved in their children's medical decisions and encourage impressionable impressionable minors to make life-altering, irreversible choices about their body.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: Let me be clear.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: Draw the line where this ideology begins to endanger women and girls.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: Biological males should not be competing in women's sports, period.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: They will always have an unfair physical advantage over biological women, no matter how they identify.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: This is reckless, irresponsible, and will be detrimental to women and children.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: New Yorkers should be represented by a council that prioritizes solving real issues for the majority over virtue signaling for a vocal minority.

utterances.speaker: Inna Vernikov
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Darlene Mealy expresses concerns regarding the modified affordability levels of the Brownsville NCP project, arguing that city-owned land should remain 100% affordable to the lowest income brackets.
chapters.title: Council Member Mealy Discusses Affordability in Brownsville NCP

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Recognizing Councilmember Amelie.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: You sure it's my turn?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: It is your turn.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: I'm just talking would like to talk about the Brownsville NCP.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: I hope all my colleagues realize once you voted against me on a project in my district, they were able to go back and change some things that I have put in.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: We should always think about this is city-owned land that we can do a hundred percent affordability.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: So now that they've um modified it, instead of 30 to 7 E AMI, it's now 40 to 60.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: I feel that area should stay 30 at least.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Affordable but affordable to who?

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: To this day, I do not know how much a three-bedroom apartment in this project is in which I have asked for.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: So please know once you start voting for projects that really do not help the community.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Some affordability.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Imagine if we we had a hundred put a hundred percent affordability.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy

chapters.summary: Council Member Crystal Hudson speaks in support of Intro 246, which requires city agencies to include an 'X' gender option on demographic forms to ensure inclusivity.
chapters.title: Council Member Hudson Discusses Gender Option Legislation

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Recognizing Councilmember Hudson.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Uh thank you so much.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Today we're voting on introduction 246.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: This bill is part of a larger package introduced by members of the LGBTQIA Plus caucus and allies in the city council aimed at strengthening protections for transgender, gender non-conforming, non-binary, and intersex individuals across New York City.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Intro 246 requires city agencies to include an ex-gender option on certain forms and mandates annual reviews to ensure demographic data collection is inclusive of sexual orientation, gender identity, and pronouns.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Eligible forms must be updated within five years, and others must be paired with a standardized anonymous survey.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I urge you all to pass intro 246 along with the entire package of bills introduced by our colleagues and proactively work to protect our transgender nonconforming and non-binary neighbors and push back against federal actions designed to disenfranchise our most vulnerable communities.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Because we know that justice for our most marginalized neighbors is justice for all New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Majority Leader Amanda Farías advocates for Intro 26A, which prohibits non-essential helicopter flights from operating out of city-owned heliports unless they meet strict FAA noise standards.
chapters.title: Majority Leader Farías Discusses Helicopter Noise Mitigation Legislation

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I'd like to take a moment to discuss my bill.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Today we're voting on legislation intro 26A, a major step forward in public safety, environmental equity, and responsible urban planning.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Intro 26 prohibits non-essential helicopter flights from operating out of city-owned teleports, unless they meet the most stringent FAA noise standards known as stage three.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: These restrictions apply to luxury sightseeing and commuter flights, which often are and use the loudest, most outdated aircrafts, and which overwhelmingly fly over neighborhoods like Lower Manhattan and Queens and in Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And let's be clear this is not about banning helicopters, it's about modernizing policy to meet the moment.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: A moment defined by urgent need to reduce emissions, protect public health, and modernize for safety.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: This bill uses the power we have, our city contracts and heliport permits, our noise code and jurisdiction to raise the bar on safety and sustainability.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Intro 26 puts into law a clear plan for stage three compliant helicopters to operate flights from our heliports.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: It also requires the economic development corporation to begin tracking and reporting on how many flights currently exceed these standards so we can hold operators accountable starting now.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: We're making a clear we're making it clear that clean air and quiet skies are not luxuries, they are necessities.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: This is climate policy, this is public health policy policy, and this is equity in action.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Thank you to my colleagues in the council for supporting this legislation.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I'd like to shout out Council members Brewers, Gennaro and Powers, and to all of the community leaders, environmental advocates, and residents who have spoken out for years about the impacts of helicopter noise and pollution.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Special thanks to Stop the Chop New York, New Jersey, who have championed this issue particularly from day one.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Um, and a last thank you to NYCEDC for their engagement throughout the entire process and to Speaker Adams for your support on moving this legislation forward.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I urge my colleagues to vote yes to curbing noise pollution in New York City with introduction 26.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: And seeing no one else signed up to speak.

chapters.summary: Council Member Diana Ayala explains her vote, sharing her perspective as a parent of public school students to demystify and defend inclusive school curriculums.
chapters.title: Council Member Ayala Explains Vote on General Orders

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Ayala.

utterances.speaker: Diana I. Ayala
utterances.text: Permission to explain my vote.

utterances.speaker: Diana I. Ayala
utterances.text: Yes, thank you.

utterances.speaker: Diana I. Ayala
utterances.text: Um, I just wanted to to chime in as well and say that, you know, I am the the parent of four children, um, and two that I've raised that have all gone to public schools, and I've never had anyone in my children come home and tell me that they were being taught a curriculum that encouraged them to be gay, trans or anything else.

utterances.speaker: Diana I. Ayala
utterances.text: Um, I think that these curriculums are important because they teach our children to be sensitive to the fact that we're all different, um, to be empathetic and to be accepting, because what I do hear from children, specifically children that are part of the LGBTQ community, is that they're often bullied and ridiculed and mistreated in our public school settings.

utterances.speaker: Diana I. Ayala
utterances.text: And so I think that you know, uh legislations like the ones that we pass here in the council take all of those things into consideration, but I want to demystify the fact that as a parent of a public school students, um, I've never ever ever heard from my children or anyone else's children that they have been taught to become gay or to uh you know behave in any such fashion.

utterances.speaker: Diana I. Ayala
utterances.text: And with that I vote aye.

chapters.summary: Council Member Eric Bottcher explains his vote, drawing parallels between past struggles for gay rights and the current fight for trans rights, urging compassion and legal safeguards.
chapters.title: Council Member Bottcher Explains Vote on General Orders

utterances.speaker: Erik D. Bottcher
utterances.text: Permission to explain my vote.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Permission granted.

utterances.speaker: Erik D. Bottcher
utterances.text: Look, it wasn't that long ago that gays and lesbians were widely to considered to be predators who were trying to recruit kids.

utterances.speaker: Erik D. Bottcher
utterances.text: And we've made a tremendous amount of progress on gay and lesbian rights.

utterances.speaker: Erik D. Bottcher
utterances.text: When I was I was born a year before homosexuality was decriminalized in New York State.

utterances.speaker: Erik D. Bottcher
utterances.text: We've come a long way.

utterances.speaker: Erik D. Bottcher
utterances.text: But one of the reasons we've been able to come a long way is because people know people got to know us as we came out.

utterances.speaker: Erik D. Bottcher
utterances.text: People knew us in their lives as their relative, as their coworker.

utterances.speaker: Erik D. Bottcher
utterances.text: And they knew that those that wasn't true, that we weren't those terrible things that were being depicted.

utterances.speaker: Erik D. Bottcher
utterances.text: The challenge with trans rights is that they're so much fewer in number that so few people know trans people in their lives.

utterances.speaker: Erik D. Bottcher
utterances.text: If they did, they would know that they were just they're just people trying to live their lives, trying to take care of themselves and their families.

utterances.speaker: Erik D. Bottcher
utterances.text: They're not trying to go out trying to recruit other people.

utterances.speaker: Erik D. Bottcher
utterances.text: It's it's a vicious, vicious stereotype.

utterances.speaker: Erik D. Bottcher
utterances.text: And look, folks don't choose to be trans no more than folks choose to be gay.

utterances.speaker: Erik D. Bottcher
utterances.text: My life would have been a lot easier.

utterances.speaker: Erik D. Bottcher
utterances.text: I'd probably be working in my father's fly fishing shop.

utterances.speaker: Erik D. Bottcher
utterances.text: I'd probably be like drinking a Miller Light on a porch somewhere right now.

utterances.speaker: Erik D. Bottcher
utterances.text: But no, I didn't have a choice.

utterances.speaker: Erik D. Bottcher
utterances.text: I am who I am.

utterances.speaker: Erik D. Bottcher
utterances.text: They are who they are.

utterances.speaker: Erik D. Bottcher
utterances.text: Let's continue to pass legislation that safeguards their rights.

utterances.speaker: Erik D. Bottcher
utterances.text: It's not complicated.

utterances.speaker: Erik D. Bottcher
utterances.text: I'm proud that's that's what we're doing in the body today.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Your vote, council member.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Your vote?

utterances.speaker: Erik D. Bottcher
utterances.text: Aye, and all.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Tiffany Cabán explains her vote, strongly defending access to gender-affirming healthcare as life-saving medical care supported by major medical associations.
chapters.title: Council Member Cabán Explains Vote on General Orders

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Caban.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Permission to explain my vote.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Permission, granted.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I will always support the transgender community.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I will not back down because the Trump administration wants to set them up as scapegoats.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I'm not gonna roll over as members of our community are villainized and demonized for who they are.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: I'm going to defend our transgender and gender nonconforming neighbors at all costs.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And I'm not defending an ideology, I'm defending lives.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Standing up to Trump and continuing to provide life-saving health care is what would actually protect young people.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: The idea that taking away health care is to protect children is false.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: The best way to protect young people is to continue to give them access to the care that they need.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: In fact, every major medical association in America supports access to gender affirming care at all ages.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Countless studies show that gender-affirming medical interventions were associated with lower odds of depression and suicidality.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: So when the Trump administration and others say that they are trying to help children, they're lying.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: They're trying to scapegoat a small population and take away their access to life-saving medical care.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: The people sounding the alarm on this are mostly brave adult parents who fear for their children's health and safety because life-saving care is being taken away from them.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And to be on other myths, gender-affirming health care has a less than 1% regret rate.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And for context, knee replacements have an up to 20% regret rate.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: Families and children's are and children are having life-saving care taken away, and that's horrible and traumatic.

utterances.speaker: Tiffany L. Cabán
utterances.text: And so I stand very proudly to say aye on that package, aye on all, uh, except for SLR one, on which I abstain.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Crystal Hudson explains her vote, sharing a personal story about a dedicated black trans woman on her staff to emphasize the human dignity of trans New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Permission to explain my vote.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Permission granted.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And I'm going to stand for this because this is deeply personal to me.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I want to share that my director of constituent services and community impact is an incredibly smart, dynamic, compassionate, and hardworking employee of the New York City Council.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: She's also a proud black trans woman.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Every day she helps families navigate the foster care system.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: She plans community engagement events for some of our most vulnerable New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: She literally keeps elders in their homes.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And these people don't care who is serving them, just as long as they're being served.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I say this with all of the love in my heart to those who could use a little bit more in theirs.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: I welcome you to come to my office and tell my director of constituent services and community impact that she doesn't matter, that her life is not worth living, that she is not deserving of the quality health care specific to her needs.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: Truly, I welcome you to come to my office and tell her that to her face.

utterances.speaker: Crystal Hudson
utterances.text: And with that, I vote aye on all.

chapters.summary: Council Member Nantasha Williams explains her vote, highlighting the importance of the trans rights package and her bill to combat deed fraud to protect homeowners.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Permission to explain my vote.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Permission granted.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: As chair of the Civil and Human Rights Committee, I truly do believe that it is our duty to protect the rights of everyone in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: And so I'm really excited to vote on this package of bills.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: I also just want to talk really briefly about another bill that is combating deed fraud.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: When a deed is recorded without a homeowner's knowledge, it's more than just an error, it's a bureaucratic failure that can cost someone their home.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: This legislation is a simple yet proactive step to clear the bureaucracy and hold the system accountable.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: This bill is a result of a close partnership with Brooklyn Legal Services Corporation A, who brought this issue forward and have represented countless clients, mostly low-income, working class and black and brown New Yorkers who own their home, who've been pushed towards foreclosure or legal hardship because they were notified in time.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: This bill closes the gap on a system that leaves homeowners vulnerable to fraud and injustice.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: Thank you, Speaker Adams and my colleagues for your continued support on this important legislation.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: We are working to ensure that our government is transparent, accountable, and truly responsive to the needs of all the people it serves.

utterances.speaker: Nantasha M. Williams
utterances.text: And with that, I vote aye on all.

utterances.text: Um, last year the council made dramatic reforms to property tax enforcement that will likely prevent many liens from being sold.

chapters.summary: Council Member Shekar Krishnan discusses Resolution 852, calling on the federal government to protect Social Security from proposed cuts and defend benefits for seniors.
chapters.title: Council Member Krishnan Discusses Resolution 852: Protecting Social Security

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Recognizing Councilmember Krishnan.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: In the last 100 years, there's not been a more powerful and treasured social program in the United States than Social Security.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: 69 million Americans rely on social security checks monthly, and over 16 million older adults are kept out of poverty thanks to this program.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: It is beyond belief that the Trump administration, Republicans in Congress have threatened to gut Social Security, a program popular amongst the majority of Americans.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: 86% of Republicans and 90% of Democrats support Social Security.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Elon Musk's Doge team has routinely made false claims and lobbied threats at the Social Security Administration to undermine confidence in the system.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: These actions jeopardize the monthly payments that our city's older adults, seniors with disabilities, and families rely on to help pay their rent, electricity, bills, and groceries.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Standing up and protecting Social Security is critical.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: We cannot cut our way to prosperity.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: We must invest in the people and communities across this country.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Today I have introduced resolution 852 to call on our partners at the federal government to do everything they can in this moment to protect social security from any cuts.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: This moment demands courage from all of us, and I can think of no worthier cause than standing up for our seniors.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: I urge my colleagues on both sides of the aisle to sign on to this resolution in support.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Great.
