chapters.summary: The Council conducts a voice vote and adopts Resolution 327, calling for state legislation to authorize retroactive property tax exemptions.
chapters.title: Voice Vote on Resolution 327: Retroactive Property Tax Exemptions

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Resolution 327 calls on New York State Legislature to pass on the governor to sign legislation allowing for property owners to receive tax exemptions to have the exemption apply retroactively where eligible.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: All those in favor say aye.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: All opposed say nay.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Any abstentions?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: The ayes have it.

chapters.summary: The Council conducts a voice vote and adopts Resolution 598, calling on healthcare institutions to contract with laundry companies that respect workers' rights.
chapters.title: Voice Vote on Resolution 598: Industrial Laundry Contracts

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I have several more folks.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: There's a there's a lull happening.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Resolution 598 calls on the New York City health care institutions to contract with industrial laundry companies that respect workers' legal rights and adhere to area standards for wages and benefits.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: All those in favor say aye.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: All opposed say nay.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Any abstentions?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: The ayes have it.

chapters.summary: The Council conducts a voice vote and adopts Resolution 771, calling on the governor to sign legislation extending protections over personal health information.
chapters.title: Voice Vote on Resolution 771: Personal Health Information Protections

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Resolution 771 calls on the New York State governor to sign S929 and A2141, which have been passed both by the New York State Senate and the New York State Assembly would extend protections over personal health information.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: All those in favor say aye.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: All opposed say nay.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Any abstentions?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: The ayes have it.

chapters.summary: The Council conducts a voice vote and adopts Resolution 774, calling for regulations requiring hospitals to adhere to WPATH standards of care.
chapters.title: Voice Vote on Resolution 774: Transgender Standards of Care in Hospitals

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Resolution 774 calls on the New York State Department of Health to implement regulations requiring hospitals in New York City to adhere to the World Professional Association for Transgender Health Standards for Care 8 to ensure medical professionals can provide high quality and ethical treatment for individuals with gender dysphoria.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: All those in favor say aye.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: All opposed say nay.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Any abstentions?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: The ayes have it.

chapters.summary: The Council conducts a voice vote and adopts Resolution 781, calling for a prohibition on out-of-state access to prescription monitoring data for certain medications.
chapters.title: Voice Vote on Resolution 781: Restricting Out-of-State Prescription Data Access

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Resolution 781 calls on the New York State legislature to introduce and pass and the governor to sign legislation to prohibit out-of-state access to the New York State prescription monitoring programs data for certain medications, including abortion medications, hormone therapy, and puberty blockers.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: All those in favor say aye.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: All opposed say nay.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Any abstentions?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: The ayes have it.

chapters.summary: The Council conducts a voice vote and adopts Resolution 793, calling for additional protections for sensitive health information related to abortion or gender-affirming care.
chapters.title: Voice Vote on Resolution 793: Sensitive Health Information Protections

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Resolution 793 calls on the New York State Legislature to pass on the governor to sign S 1633 slash A2613, which would provide additional protections for the sensitive health information of patients, including information related to abortion or gender-affirming care, and require all health information networks, electronic health record systems, and health care providers to provide patients with a right to restrict the disclosures of such patients' health information.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: All those in favor say aye.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: All opposed say nay.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Any abstentions?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: The ayes have it.

chapters.summary: During general discussion, Council Member Gail Brewer pays tribute to Barry Benepe, the co-founder of the Greenmarket, who passed away at age 96, highlighting his contributions to the city.
chapters.title: Council Member Brewer Pays Tribute to Barry Benepe

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: We will now move into general discussion, and I'd like to first recognize Councilmember Brewer, followed by Holden, then Sanchez.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I'm here to talk about Barry Bannapee who died yesterday.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: He was the head of the founder of the Green Market, the co-founder of the Green Market.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: He died at 96.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: He was also a architectural and brilliant writer on so many different topics.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: He was somebody who I think uh Jane Jacobs was a friend of.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: He wrote about um the parks, the bikes, um, how to make our neighborhoods a better place.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Obviously, his son, Adrian Benepee, some of you know as the parks commissioner and now head of Brooklyn Botanical.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Um, his Barry Benepee's father and my grandfather were best friends for 80 years.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And he was somebody, I think, who made this city a much better place.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So we obviously um wish his uh wife, Judith Becktor, who was a friend of mine even before she married him and his amazing family, very talented family in many ways, um, send our condolences.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I I think the people like Barry don't come along very often.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: He ha was a graduate of very prestigious colleges and graduate schools and decided that he wanted the farmers in the city of New York and the state of New York to get their due.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And the way to do it was to start the 14th Street Green Market.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And I think all of us can thank him because many of us go to the green market, and please think of them every time you do so.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I'd like to read a correction into the record on the votes on introduction 246A, which was adopted by a vote of 43 in the affirmative four in the negative and two abstentions.

chapters.summary: Council Member Robert Holden introduces legislation to strengthen enforcement against fire hydrant obstruction and to ban inhumane animal practices in rodeos.
chapters.title: Council Member Holden Introduces Fire Hydrant and Rodeo Animal Protection Bills

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I'd now like to recognize Councilmember Holden, followed by Sanchez then Lee.

utterances.speaker: Robert F. Holden
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Robert F. Holden
utterances.text: Today I introduced two pieces of legislation: intro 1255 and intro 1256 that reflect our ongoing commitment to public safety and compassion.

utterances.speaker: Robert F. Holden
utterances.text: One bill, intro 1256, strengthens enforcement against the dangerous and irresponsible practice of obstructing fire hydrants, not just by parking in front of them, but by covering them with garbage bins or other objects to conceal their location.

utterances.speaker: Robert F. Holden
utterances.text: These tactics endanger the lives uh by uh delaying emergency response when every second counts.

utterances.speaker: Robert F. Holden
utterances.text: The other bill, intro 1255, takes a stand against the abuse of animals in rodeos, banning inhumane practices like calf roping, electric prods, and flank straps right here in New York City.

utterances.speaker: Robert F. Holden
utterances.text: Animals should not suffer for the sake of human entertainment.

utterances.speaker: Robert F. Holden
utterances.text: These are common sense quality of life bills.

utterances.speaker: Robert F. Holden
utterances.text: Governments should focus on the issues that directly affect our residents, and I believe both bills will do that.

utterances.speaker: Robert F. Holden
utterances.text: Whether it's protecting critical firefighting infrastructure or standing up for animals who can't speak for themselves, this kind of practical leadership, this is the kind of practical leadership New Yorkers expect and deserve.

utterances.speaker: Robert F. Holden
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Pierina Sanchez introduces a package of three bills aimed at improving the Housing Connect application system, reducing wait times, and providing assistance to applicants.
chapters.title: Council Member Sanchez Introduces Housing Connect Improvement Bills

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Recognizing Councilmember Sanchez, followed by Lee, then Krishna.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Uh, between January of 2014 and June of 2020, six-year period, we had 29 million applications received by Housing Connect compared to 27,819 leases signed.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: For as frustrating for as frustrating as it is to search for an affordable apartment in New York City, it's made all the more frustrating by application technology that sometimes causes unnecessary issues.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Over the last two years, my office has been collaborating with housing providers and housing lottery applicants to develop solutions to problems that plague Housing Connect.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And to that end, I'm proud to introduce three bills today that I hope my colleagues will sign on to.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: 1264 addresses the issue of prolonged and onerous wait times to fill re-rentals.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: 1265 ensures applicants or a designee, that's a new thing, get text and email updates about their applications, and 1266 would create a free weekly in-person Housing Connect assistance program in certain public locations.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: The housing lottery should help people to find uh pathways to affordable housing, not be hindrances, hindrances.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: And so I would like to thank the team at UNHP and the members of Aura, as well as the members of my staff for working so hard on this bill, and of course to my colleagues who are co-priming this.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank thank you to all of you.

utterances.speaker: Pierina Ana Sanchez
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Linda Lee introduces Intro 1259, which requires direct service staff in juvenile detention facilities to receive training in behavioral support and de-escalation strategies.
chapters.title: Council Member Lee Introduces Juvenile Detention Staff Training Bill

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Recognizing Councilmember Lee, followed by Krishna, then Mealy.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Good afternoon, colleagues.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Today I'm proud to introduce legislation that takes a necessary step towards ensuring the safety, dignity, and well-being of young people in our juvenile detention system.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Intro 1259 requires that direct service staff, those who interact most closely with youth in our detention facilities, receive foundational training and behavioral support strategies.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: These are the staff at detention centers and community-based organizations who are with these young people day in and day out, managing tense moments, building trust, and shaping outcomes.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Right now, many of these workers do not have the tools they need, and they may not have formal training in de-escalation, trauma-informed care, or culturally competent behavioral responses.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: And when a moment of crisis occurs, they're left without clear evidence-based strategies to handle it.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: That's what this bill changes.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: We're requiring that all direct service staff complete training in the basic science of behavior and learning, techniques for reinforcing positive behaviors, non-punitive de-escalation methods, and trauma-informed culturally responsive approaches to care.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: The training will be flexible, it can be done in person, online, or through a hybrid model, and it will be mandatory for all current staff within a year with a clear timeline for new hires.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: This is about leveling the playing field by investing not only in government systems but also in the trusted community partners who make rehabilitation truly possible.

utterances.speaker: Linda Lee
utterances.text: Thank you, and I look forward to working with all of you to see this passed.

chapters.summary: Council Member Shekar Krishnan reiterates the importance of protecting Social Security and urges colleagues to support his resolution calling on the federal government to prevent cuts.
chapters.title: Council Member Krishnan Discusses Resolution to Protect Social Security

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Recognizing Councilmember Krishnan, followed by Mealy.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thank you, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: We're going to do a round two over here.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: In the last 100 years, there has not been a more powerful and treasured social program in the United States than Social Security.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Sixty-nine million Americans rely on Social Security check monthly, and over 16 million older adults are kept out of poverty thanks to this critical program.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: It is beyond belief that the Trump administration and Republicans in Congress have threatened to gut Social Security, a program popular amongst the majority of Americans.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: 86% of Republicans and 90% of Democrats support Social Security.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Elon Musk's Doge team has routinely made false claims and lobbied threats at the Social Security Administration to undermine confidence in the system.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: These actions jeopardize the monthly payments that our city's older adults, seniors who are disabled, and families rely on to help pay their rent, electricity bills, and groceries.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Today I've introduced resolution 852 to call on our partners at the federal government to do everything they can to protect Social Security from these drastic cuts.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: This moment demands courage from all of us, and I can think of no worthier cause than standing up for our seniors, our individuals with disabilities, our communities, and the many others who rely on Social Security.

utterances.speaker: Shekar Krishnan
utterances.text: Thank you.

chapters.summary: Council Member Darlene Mealy welcomes newly elected Council Member Simcha Felder back to the Council and congratulates Kalman Yeger on his transition to the State Assembly.
chapters.title: Council Member Mealy Welcomes Simcha Felder and Kalman Yeger

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Recognizing Councilmember Milley.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Is it my time?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Yes.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: I just want to bring my support.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: I would like to uh welcome Sumptafelder, my former colleague into City Council.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: And I wasn't able to uh say bye to uh Compton Yeager, so congratulations to the assembly, and we're looking forward.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: And welcome to the House party.

utterances.speaker: Darlene Mealy
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Seeing no one else signed up to speak.

chapters.summary: Speaker Adrienne Adams formally adjourns the stated meeting of April 24, 2025.
chapters.title: Adjournment

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I'll now call on Speaker Adrian Adams to close today's stated meeting.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: And I will do just that, Majority Leader.

utterances.speaker: Adrienne E. Adams
utterances.text: The stated meeting of April 24th, 2025 is hereby adjourned.

summary: The Council passed a comprehensive legislative package aimed at protecting and expanding rights for transgender, gender nonconforming, and nonbinary New Yorkers, which sparked intense debate among members.
Legislation was approved to mitigate helicopter noise pollution by restricting non-essential flights at city heliports that do not meet strict FAA noise standards.
The Council voted on various land use, finance, and environmental measures, including the Brownsville NCP affordable housing project and stormwater management plans.
title: Stated Meeting
